questions.prompt: Complete the inverted sentence: "___ have I seen such determination."
questions.explanation: Inversion is triggered by a negative or restrictive adverbial placed at the front. Only "Rarely" is a negative adverbial that triggers auxiliary inversion. "Often", "Sometimes", and "Usually" are positive adverbials and do not trigger inversion.

questions.prompt: Complete: "No sooner had the match ended ___ the crowd rushed onto the pitch."
questions.explanation: The fixed pairing is No sooner…than. "when" goes with Hardly/Scarcely, "that" is used in result clauses (so…that), and "but" belongs to Not only…but also. No sooner always partners with than.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Hardly ___ I arrived when the director called me into the office."
questions.explanation: The main verb arrived is in the past simple, so the correct auxiliary in the inverted clause is had (past perfect): Hardly had I arrived when… The past perfect is used because this event happened just before another past event. "have" is present perfect, "did" is past simple, and "was" doesn't fit.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses inversion with 'Not only'?

options.text: Not only she won the prize, but she also gave a speech.
Not only did she win the prize, but she also gave a speech.
Not only did she won the prize, but she also gave a speech.
Not only she did win the prize, but she also gave a speech.

questions.explanation: After 'Not only', inversion is required: auxiliary (did) before subject (she), then the base verb (win): Not only did she win… Option (a) has no inversion, (c) wrongly uses the past participle won after did, and (d) puts the subject before the auxiliary.

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ no circumstances should you share your login details."
questions.explanation: The fixed expression that triggers inversion is "Under no circumstances": Under no circumstances should you… It is always "under" — not in, on, or at. This expression is used in formal notices to mean "in no situation whatsoever".
